annotations.label: Directed acyclic graph (DAG)

anchor.snippet: a directed acyclic graph ( DAG ) is a directed graph with no directed cycles

annotations.note: Directed graphs are formalized as `Digraph` (and `Quiver`), but Mathlib has no acyclicity/no-directed-cycles predicate for them, so the DAG notion itself is absent.

annotations.label: DAG iff topologically orderable

anchor.snippet: A directed graph is a DAG if and only if it can be topologically ordered

annotations.note: Neither DAGs nor topological orderings exist in Mathlib, so this equivalence is not formalized.

annotations.label: Walk in a directed graph

anchor.snippet: A walk in a directed graph is a (finite or infinite) sequence

annotations.note: `Quiver.Path` is an inductive type of finite composable-arrow sequences (a finite directed walk, vertices may repeat), but only finite walks are covered.

annotations.label: Path

anchor.snippet: A path is a walk where all vertices are distinct

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.Walk.IsPath` is the distinct-vertices predicate but only for undirected graphs; no directed-path-with-distinct-vertices predicate exists.

annotations.label: Cycle

anchor.snippet: A cycle is a walk

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.Walk.IsCycle` formalizes cycles for undirected graphs only; there is no directed-cycle notion.

annotations.label: Directed acyclic graph (no cycles)

anchor.snippet: A directed acyclic graph is a directed graph that has no cycles

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.IsAcyclic` (no cyclic walks) is the acyclicity predicate but only for undirected graphs, not directed ones.

annotations.label: Reachability relation as partial order

anchor.section: Reachability relation, transitive closure, and transitive reduction
anchor.snippet: The reachability relation of a DAG can be formalized as a partial order

annotations.note: `Relation.ReflTransGen` is the reflexive-transitive closure (reachability) of any relation giving a preorder, but the DAG-specific antisymmetry making it a partial order is not proven.

annotations.label: Distinct DAGs, same reachability

anchor.section: Reachability relation, transitive closure, and transitive reduction
anchor.snippet: different DAGs may give rise to the same reachability relation

annotations.note: No formalization of DAGs or this non-injectivity-of-reachability observation exists.

annotations.label: Transitive closure of a DAG

anchor.section: Reachability relation, transitive closure, and transitive reduction
anchor.snippet: The transitive closure of a DAG is the graph with the most edges that has the same reachability relation as the DAG

annotations.note: `Relation.TransGen` is the transitive closure of an arbitrary relation, capturing the construction, but not the graph-theoretic 'maximum edges' characterization.

annotations.label: Finite poset representable as DAG

anchor.section: Reachability relation, transitive closure, and transitive reduction
anchor.snippet: every finite partially ordered set can be represented as a DAG

annotations.note: There is no DAG type nor a theorem representing finite posets as DAGs.

annotations.label: Transitive reduction of a DAG

anchor.section: Reachability relation, transitive closure, and transitive reduction
anchor.snippet: The transitive reduction of a DAG is the graph with the fewest edges that has the same reachability relation as the DAG

annotations.note: The covering relation `CovBy` (⋖) is the transitive reduction for a (finite) partial order, but a general transitive-reduction operation on directed graphs is not formalized.

annotations.label: Hasse diagram

anchor.section: Reachability relation, transitive closure, and transitive reduction
anchor.snippet: A Hasse diagram of a partial order is a drawing of the transitive reduction

annotations.note: `SimpleGraph.hasse` is the Hasse diagram of an order as the graph of its covering relation (the combinatorial object, not the geometric drawing).

annotations.label: Topological ordering

anchor.snippet: A topological ordering of a directed graph is an ordering of its vertices into a sequence

annotations.note: No notion of topological ordering of a directed graph is present in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Graph with topological ordering is acyclic

anchor.snippet: every graph with a topological ordering is acyclic

annotations.note: Topological orderings of directed graphs are not formalized, so this implication is absent.

annotations.label: Every DAG has a topological ordering

anchor.snippet: every directed acyclic graph has at least one topological ordering

annotations.note: The order-theoretic analogue (Szpilrajn) `extend_partialOrder`, that every partial order extends to a linear order, exists, but the graph statement about DAGs is not stated.

annotations.label: Unique topological ordering condition

anchor.snippet: a DAG has a unique topological ordering if and only if it has a directed path containing all the vertices

annotations.note: Neither topological orderings nor this Hamiltonian-path uniqueness criterion are formalized.

annotations.label: Topological orderings are linear extensions

anchor.snippet: The family of topological orderings of a DAG is the same as the family of linear extensions

annotations.note: `LinearExtension` formalizes linear extensions of a partial order, but the equivalence with topological orderings of a DAG is not stated (topological orderings being absent).

annotations.label: Number of labeled DAGs and recurrence

anchor.snippet: The number of DAGs on n labeled vertices

annotations.note: No enumeration of labeled DAGs or its recurrence is formalized.

annotations.label: DAGs and positive-eigenvalue (0,1) matrices

anchor.snippet: a matrix A is an adjacency matrix of a DAG if and only if A + I is a (0,1) matrix with all eigenvalues positive

annotations.note: This adjacency-matrix eigenvalue characterization of DAGs is not formalized.

annotations.label: Multitree

anchor.snippet: is a DAG in which there is at most one directed path between any two vertices

annotations.note: The multitree (diamond-free poset / strongly unambiguous DAG) notion is not in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Polytree

anchor.snippet: is a multitree formed by orienting the edges of an undirected tree

annotations.note: Polytrees (orientations of undirected trees) are not formalized.

annotations.label: Arborescence

anchor.snippet: An arborescence is a polytree formed by orienting the edges of an undirected tree away from a particular vertex

annotations.note: `Quiver.Arborescence` is exactly a directed rooted tree: a root with a unique directed path to every vertex.

annotations.label: Topological sorting in linear time

anchor.snippet: Topological sorting is the algorithmic problem of finding a topological ordering of a given DAG

annotations.note: No topological sorting algorithm or its complexity is formalized.

annotations.label: DAG recognition in linear time

anchor.snippet: It is also possible to check whether a given directed graph is a DAG in linear time

annotations.note: DAG recognition and its linear-time complexity are not formalized.

annotations.label: Acyclic orientation

anchor.snippet: Any undirected graph may be made into a DAG by choosing a total order for its vertices

annotations.note: Mathlib's `Digraph.Orientation` only forgets orientation (digraph→simple graph); constructing an acyclic orientation from a total order is not formalized (and is listed as a TODO).

annotations.label: Acyclic orientations and chromatic polynomial

anchor.snippet: The number of acyclic orientations is equal to

annotations.note: Neither acyclic orientations nor the chromatic polynomial exist in Mathlib, so Stanley's theorem is absent.

annotations.label: Feedback vertex/arc set removal

anchor.snippet: Any directed graph may be made into a DAG by removing a feedback vertex set or a feedback arc set

annotations.note: Feedback vertex/arc sets are not formalized.

annotations.label: Condensation

anchor.snippet: An arbitrary directed graph may also be transformed into a DAG, called its condensation

annotations.note: Strongly connected components and the condensation construction are not formalized.

annotations.label: Transitive closure construction time

anchor.snippet: may be constructed in time O ( mn )

annotations.note: Algorithmic time bounds for transitive closure are not formalized.

annotations.label: Transitive reduction construction time

anchor.snippet: the transitive reduction can be constructed in the same asymptotic time bounds as the transitive closure

annotations.note: Algorithmic time bounds for transitive reduction are not formalized.

annotations.label: Closure problem

anchor.snippet: The closure problem takes as input a vertex-weighted directed acyclic graph

annotations.note: The closure problem (max-weight closed set) is not formalized.

annotations.label: Shortest and longest paths in DAGs

anchor.snippet: it is possible to find shortest paths and longest paths from a given starting vertex in DAGs in linear time

annotations.note: Linear-time shortest/longest path algorithms on DAGs are not formalized.

annotations.label: Dependency graph

anchor.snippet: a dependency graph is a graph that has a vertex for each object to be updated

annotations.note: Dependency graphs (an application notion) are not formalized.

annotations.label: Circular dependency

anchor.snippet: A cycle in this graph is called a circular dependency

annotations.note: Circular dependencies are not formalized.

annotations.label: Critical path (PERT)

anchor.snippet: The longest path in this DAG represents the critical path of the project

annotations.note: PERT critical-path analysis is not formalized.

annotations.label: DAG as processing network

anchor.snippet: A directed acyclic graph may be used to represent a network of processing elements

annotations.note: Processing-network applications of DAGs are not formalized.

annotations.label: Acyclic dependencies principle

anchor.snippet: the acyclic dependencies principle states that the dependencies between modules or components of a large software system should form a directed acyclic graph

annotations.note: This software-engineering principle is not formalized.

annotations.label: Feedforward neural networks

anchor.snippet: Feedforward neural networks are another example

annotations.note: Feedforward neural networks are not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Bayesian network

anchor.snippet: a Bayesian network represents a system of probabilistic events as vertices in a directed acyclic graph

annotations.note: Bayesian networks are not formalized.

annotations.label: Moral graph of a DAG

anchor.snippet: the moral graph of a DAG is the undirected graph created by adding an (undirected) edge between all parents of the same vertex

annotations.note: The moralization/moral graph construction is not formalized.

annotations.label: Influence diagram

anchor.snippet: the vertices of which represent either decisions to be made or unknown information

annotations.note: Influence diagrams are not formalized.

annotations.label: DAGs encode causal structure

anchor.snippet: in any application represented by a directed acyclic graph there is a causal structure

annotations.note: Causal-structure semantics of DAGs are not formalized.

annotations.label: Family trees as DAGs

anchor.snippet: Family trees may be seen as directed acyclic graphs

annotations.note: Genealogical DAGs are not formalized.

annotations.label: Version history (Git)

anchor.snippet: The version history of a distributed revision control system, such as Git

annotations.note: Version-control history DAGs are not formalized.

annotations.label: History DAG (Delaunay triangulation)

anchor.snippet: the algorithm maintains a history DAG representing the version history of a geometric structure

annotations.note: History DAGs for incremental geometric algorithms are not formalized.

annotations.label: Citation graph

anchor.snippet: In a citation graph the vertices are documents with a single publication date

annotations.note: Citation graphs are not formalized.

annotations.label: Longest path scaling in Price model

anchor.snippet: the length of the longest path, from the n-th node added to the network to the first node in the network, scales as

annotations.note: The Price-model longest-path scaling result is not formalized.

annotations.label: DAG as compact sequence representation

anchor.snippet: Directed acyclic graphs may also be used as a compact representation of a collection of sequences

annotations.note: Using DAGs to compactly represent sequence collections is not formalized.

annotations.label: Directed acyclic word graph

anchor.snippet: the directed acyclic word graph is a data structure in computer science formed by a directed acyclic graph with a single source

annotations.note: The DAWG data structure is not formalized.

annotations.label: Trie

anchor.snippet: the tree formed in this way for a set of strings is called a trie

annotations.note: The trie data structure is not formalized in Mathlib.

annotations.label: Binary search tree as rooted DAG

anchor.snippet: a binary search tree can be viewed as a rooted DAG where paths represent sorted orderings of keys

annotations.note: This DAG view of binary search trees is not formalized (Mathlib has ordered-set trees in `Data.Ordmap` but not this framing).

annotations.label: Binary decision diagram

anchor.snippet: a DAG-based data structure for representing binary functions

annotations.note: Binary decision diagrams are not formalized.
